title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Credit Trading Rise, While Prices See Mixed Trends
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(10th March â€“ 16th March)
  • A total of 294,000 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 65.30% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a rise of 37.70% WoW as 1,531,331 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $57.40/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording an 0.24%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 12.46% in price, reaching $60.02/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price increased by 3.19% WoW; on 16th March, the spot price stood at $60.00/MT and the benchmark price stood at $64.75/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices decreased by 2.04% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 2.74% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

CA LCFS credit trading saw an increase over the past week, with both Type 1 and non-zero credit transactions rising, while prices showed mixed movementsâ€”Type 1 credits remained relatively stable with a slight decline, whereas non-zero transfer prices dropped more noticeably. The LCFS spot price, however, edged higher, with the benchmark price holding firm. On the policy front,Â the Washington Department of Ecology extended its Clean Fuel Standard Rulemaking timeline. Federal lawmakers in U.S. pushed for year-round sales of higher ethanol blends and trade associations expected the removal of trade barriers for U.S. ethanol exports. CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend [caption id="attachment_593968" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.24% WoW fall, decreasing from $57.54 to $57.40 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 12.46% WoW, falling from $68.56 to $60.02. Type 1 trades saw 294,000 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 65.30% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a increase of 37.70% to reach 1,513,331 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_593969" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 3.19% WoW, reaching $58.18/MT. On 18th March, the average spot price was $60.00/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $64.75/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_593970" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 75 trades were reported in the market, with 14 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $77.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 3,000 and 85,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $44.15 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 32,407 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 2,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $65.11. The largest volume transfers of 300,000 credits of the Type 2 category were undertaken for a price of $50.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_593971" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 spot price in February stood at $72.15, with total trading volumes reaching 6.31 MMT. This marks an 18.22% year-over-year increase from February 2024, when the average spot price was $61.03 and volumes totalled 1.96 MMT. By March 16, the Type 1 average spot price had dropped to $57.45, falling below February levels, while trading volumes reached 2.61 MMT.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_593972" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 10th March â€“ 16th March, total VPT recorded a 46.00% decrease (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices decreased by 2.04% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 2.74% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Important Update to Clean Fuel Standard Rulemaking Timeline. The Washington Department of Ecology is extending the timeline of the Clean Fuel Standard Rulemaking as the state legislature considers bills that could affect the program. The proposal was scheduled to occur by mid-March, and we now expect to file the proposal several weeks following the current legislative session, which is scheduled to end on April 27.
  • Growth Energy Urges USTR to Tear Down U.S. Ethanol Export Barriers. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, offered the Trump administration a list of foreign trade barriers that should be targeted for elimination by the U.S. Trade Representative (USTR). Growth Energy shared the information as part of comments filed in response to the America First Trade Policy Presidential Memorandum and the Presidential Memorandum on Reciprocal Trade and Tariffs.
  • Fischer, Smith Urge Congress to Fulfill President Trumpâ€™s Pledge to Allow Year-Round Sale of E15.S. Senator Deb Fischer (R-Neb.), a member of the Senate Agriculture Committee, joined U.S. Representative Adrian Smith (NE-03) at a press conference urging Congress to fulfill President Trumpâ€™s pledge to allow the sale of year-round E15. During her remarks, Senator Fischer called on Congress to pass her Nationwide Consumer and Fuel Retailer Choice Act of 2025, ending years of patchwork regulations and giving producers and consumers the certainty they deserve. Senator Fischer and Congressman Smith were joined by National Corn Growers Association President Kenneth Hartman Jr., along with U.S. Representatives Nikki Budzinski (IL-13), Ashley Hinson (IA-02), Mike Flood (NE-01), and Jim Baird (IN-04).
  • EPA Announces Action to Implement POTUSâ€™s Termination of Biden-Harris Electric Vehicle Mandate.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin announced the agency will reconsider the Model Year 2027 and Later Light-Duty and Medium-Duty Vehicles regulation and Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards for Heavy-Duty Vehicles. In addition to imposing over $700 billion in regulatory and compliance costs, these rules provided the foundation for the Biden-Harris electric vehicle mandate that takes away Americansâ€™ ability to choose a safe and affordable car for their family and increases the cost of living on all products that trucks deliver.
